request.question: The Force India VJM09 is a Formula One racing car designed by Force India, to compete in the 2016 Formula One season, the car was driven by which Le Mans winner, a German professional racing driver currently racing for the Renault Sport F1 Team?

title: Force India VJM09
Giancarlo Fisichella
Force India VJM05
Force India VJM07
Nico Hülkenberg
Lotus E20
Force India VJM04
Force India VJM08
Renault R29
Renault R.S.16
sentences: The Force India VJM09 is a Formula One racing car designed by Force India to compete in the 2016 Formula One season.
The car was driven by Le Mans winner Nico Hülkenberg and Sergio Pérez, and used the Mercedes PU106C Hybrid power unit.
Giancarlo Fisichella (] ; born 14 January 1973), also known as Fisico, Giano or Fisi, is an Italian professional racing driver.
He has driven in Formula One for Minardi, Jordan, Benetton, Sauber, Renault, Force India and Ferrari.
Since then he has driven for AF Corse in their Ferrari 458 GTE at various sportscar events, becoming twice a Le Mans 24 Hour class winner, and a GT class winner of the Petit Le Mans at Road Atlanta.
He was also Ferrari's F1 reserve driver for 2010.
The Force India VJM05 is a Formula One racing car designed by Sahara Force India F1 Team for use in the 2012 Formula One season.
The car was launched on 3 February at Silverstone.
It was driven by Paul di Resta, and Nico Hülkenberg who returned to the sport after spending one year as Force India's test and reserve driver.
The Force India VJM07 is a Formula One racing car designed by Force India to compete in the 2014 Formula One season.
It was driven by Nico Hülkenberg, who returned to the team after racing for Sauber in 2013, and Sergio Pérez, who joined the team after leaving McLaren.
The VJM07 was designed to use Mercedes' new 1.6-litre V6 turbocharged engine, the PU106A Hybrid.
Nicolas Hülkenberg (] , born 19 August 1987) is a German professional racing driver currently racing for the Renault Sport F1 Team.
In 2015, he also contested in two rounds of the 2015 FIA World Endurance Championship season for Porsche, winning the 2015 24 Hours of Le Mans in his first attempt.
He was the 2009 champion of the GP2 Series, and is a previous champion of both the Formula 3 Euro Series and A1 Grand Prix, as part of A1 Team Germany.
He is one of three drivers since 2005 to win the GP2 series championship in his debut season, the others being Lewis Hamilton and Nico Rosberg.

s of September 2017 , Hülkenberg holds the record for the most Formula One career starts without a podium finish, a record he broke when he failed to finish in his 129th race (the 2017 Singapore Grand Prix) and in so doing passed Adrian Sutil's previous record of 128.
The Lotus E20 is a Formula One racing car designed and produced by the Enstone-based Lotus F1 Team for the 2012 Formula One season.
The E20 was the twentieth Formula One car to be designed at Enstone since 1992, and was named in tribute to the contribution made by the facility and its personnel in their twenty-year history.
The car was also the first from Enstone to carry the Lotus name, since the team ceased using the Renault name following their renaming from Lotus Renault GP at the end of 2011.
The team continued to use Renault engines for the car.

The car, which was launched by the team on its website on 5 February 2012, was driven by 2007 World Drivers' Champion Kimi Räikkönen and 2011 GP2 Series champion Romain Grosjean, both of whom returned to Formula One after a two-year absence.
The Force India VJM04 is a Formula One racing car developed by Force India for the 2011 Formula One season, the fourth car the team has made since entering the sport in 2008.
The car was driven by long-time Force India driver Adrian Sutil and 2010 Deutsche Tourenwagen Masters champion Paul di Resta.
The car was launched online on 8 February 2011.
The Force India VJM08 is a Formula One racing car which Force India used to compete in the 2015 Formula One season.
It was driven by Sergio Pérez and Le Mans winner Nico Hülkenberg.

The Renault R29 is a Formula One racing car designed by the Renault F1 Team, with which they contested the 2009 Formula One World Championship (originally as ING Renault F1 Team, then, following loss of title sponsorship from the ING Group after "Crashgate", Renault F1 Team).
Coming off a resurgent second half of the previous year, the team expected to be fighting for both the drivers and constructors titles.
Instead, the car proved to be disappointing.
Fernando Alonso scored all of the team's 26 points.
In the hands of either of Alonso's team mates, the car was often towards the back of the field with Nelson Piquet, Jr. and Romain Grosjean often failing to reach Q2.

Unlike many other 2009 cars that began the year uncompetitively, such as the McLaren MP4-24, the R29 showed no sign of improvement throughout the season, and arguably became less competitive over the course of the year, despite Alonso's third place in Singapore, which was Renault's only podium finish of the season.

The Renault R.S.16 is a Formula One racing car designed by the Renault Sport Formula One Team to compete in the 2016 Formula One season, and marks Renault's return to the sport as a constructor after a five-year absence.
The car was built in Enstone in Oxfordshire with the engine supplied from Viry-Châtillon in France.
It was driven by former McLaren driver Kevin Magnussen and 2014 GP2 Series champion Jolyon Palmer.
Renault chairman Carlos Ghosn declared that they would not win in 2016, but wanted to in the future.
The Renault R.S.16 was originally based on the Lotus E23 Hybrid car despite previously sported the Mercedes power units.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: The Force India VJM09 is a Formula One racing car designed by Force India, to compete in the 2016 Formula One season, the car was driven by which Le Mans winner, a German professional racing driver currently racing for the Renault Sport F1 Team?
hop1_docs: Force India VJM09 | The Force India VJM09 is a Formula One racing car designed by Force India to compete in the 2016 Formula One season.  The car was driven by Le Mans winner Nico Hülkenberg and Sergio Pérez, and used the Mercedes PU106C Hybrid power unit.

Force India VJM05 | The Force India VJM05 is a Formula One racing car designed by Sahara Force India F1 Team for use in the 2012 Formula One season.  The car was launched on 3 February at Silverstone.  It was driven by Paul di Resta, and Nico Hülkenberg who returned to the sport after spending one year as Force India's test and reserve driver.

Force India VJM08 | The Force India VJM08 is a Formula One racing car which Force India used to compete in the 2015 Formula One season.  It was driven by Sergio Pérez and Le Mans winner Nico Hülkenberg.

Force India VJM04 | The Force India VJM04 is a Formula One racing car developed by Force India for the 2011 Formula One season, the fourth car the team has made since entering the sport in 2008.  The car was driven by long-time Force India driver Adrian Sutil and 2010 Deutsche Tourenwagen Masters champion Paul di Resta.  The car was launched online on 8 February 2011.

Force India VJM10 | The Force India VJM10 is a Formula One racing car designed and constructed by Force India to compete during the 2017 Formula One season.  The car is driven by Sergio Pérez and Esteban Ocon, who joined the team after Nico Hülkenberg left the team at the end of the season.  It made its competitive début at the 2017 Australian Grand Prix.

Force India VJM07 | The Force India VJM07 is a Formula One racing car designed by Force India to compete in the 2014 Formula One season.  It was driven by Nico Hülkenberg, who returned to the team after racing for Sauber in 2013, and Sergio Pérez, who joined the team after leaving McLaren.  The VJM07 was designed to use Mercedes' new 1.6-litre V6 turbocharged engine, the PU106A Hybrid.

Red Bull RB10 | The Red Bull RB10 is a Formula One racing car designed by Adrian Newey for Infiniti Red Bull Racing to compete in the 2014 Formula One season.  It was driven by reigning World Drivers' Champion Sebastian Vettel and Daniel Ricciardo, who was promoted from sister team Scuderia Toro Rosso after Mark Webber announced his retirement from the sport at the end of the 2013 season.  The RB10 was designed to use Renault Sport's new 1.6-litre V6 turbocharged engine, the Renault Energy F1-2014.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Force India VJM09 — [1]: "The Force India VJM09 is a Formula One racing car"
- Nico Hülkenberg — [1]: "Le Mans winner Nico Hülkenberg" ; [3]: "driven by Sergio Pérez and Le Mans winner Nico Hülkenberg." ; [6]: "It was driven by Nico Hülkenberg"
- Sergio Pérez — [1]: "Sergio Pérez" ; [3]: "driven by Sergio Pérez and Le Mans winner Nico Hülkenberg."

Ambiguities:
- No ambiguity for the named Le Mans winner: "Nico Hülkenberg" appears consistently.
- The phrase "Renault Sport F1 Team" (as in the question) does not appear in the provided passages.

Bridge / missing fact:
- The passages do not state Nico Hülkenberg's current team affiliation (e.g., whether he is "currently racing for the Renault Sport F1 Team").

Retrieval direction:
- Open page: "Nico Hülkenberg" (Wikipedia page) to confirm current team affiliation and biography.
- Open page: "Renault F1 Team" (or "Renault in Formula One"/"Renault Sport F1 Team") to verify roster and Hülkenberg's association.
- Run query: "Nico Hülkenberg currently racing for Renault Sport F1 Team" (exact search).
Conclusion: The VJM09 was driven by Le Mans winner Nico Hülkenberg.
response.hop2_query: Nico Hülkenberg Renault Sport F1 Team
hop2_docs: Renault R.S.17 | The Renault R.S.17 is a Formula One racing car designed and constructed by the Renault Sport Formula One Team to compete during the 2017 Formula One season.  The car is driven by Nico Hülkenberg and Jolyon Palmer.  Hülkenberg joined the team after Kevin Magnussen left the team at the end of the season.  It made its competitive début at the 2017 Australian Grand Prix.  Renault R.S.17 was the first car to be fueled by BP and lubricated by Castrol since Marussia MR02 car despite Cosworth had a technical relationship with BP-Castrol.

Nico Hülkenberg | Nicolas Hülkenberg (] , born 19 August 1987) is a German professional racing driver currently racing for the Renault Sport F1 Team.  In 2015, he also contested in two rounds of the 2015 FIA World Endurance Championship season for Porsche, winning the 2015 24 Hours of Le Mans in his first attempt.  He was the 2009 champion of the GP2 Series, and is a previous champion of both the Formula 3 Euro Series and A1 Grand Prix, as part of A1 Team Germany.  He is one of three drivers since 2005 to win the GP2 series championship in his debut season, the others being Lewis Hamilton and Nico Rosberg.  s of September 2017 , Hülkenberg holds the record for the most Formula One career starts without a podium finish, a record he broke when he failed to finish in his 129th race (the 2017 Singapore Grand Prix) and in so doing passed Adrian Sutil's previous record of 128.

Force India VJM05 | The Force India VJM05 is a Formula One racing car designed by Sahara Force India F1 Team for use in the 2012 Formula One season.  The car was launched on 3 February at Silverstone.  It was driven by Paul di Resta, and Nico Hülkenberg who returned to the sport after spending one year as Force India's test and reserve driver.

Lotus F1 | Lotus F1 Team was a British Formula One racing team.  The team competed under the Lotus name from 2012 until 2015, following the renaming of the former Renault team based at Enstone in Oxfordshire.  The Lotus F1 Team was majority owned by Genii Capital.  Lotus F1 was named after its branding partner Group Lotus.  The team achieved a race victory and fourth position in the Formula One Constructors' World Championship in their first season under the Lotus title.  The team was sold back to Renault on 18 December 2015, The Lotus F1 Team name was officially dropped on 3 February 2016, and announced that they would compete as Renault Sport Formula One Team.

Renault Formula One crash controversy | The Renault Formula One crash controversy, dubbed Crashgate by some in the media, is a sporting scandal perpetrated by the Renault F1 team who ordered Nelson Piquet Jr. to crash deliberately during the 2008 Singapore Grand Prix, to gain a sporting advantage for his Renault teammate Fernando Alonso.

Jolyon Palmer | Jolyon Palmer (born 20 January 1991) is a British racing driver, currently driving for Renault Sport F1 Team in the Formula One World Championship.  Palmer stayed with Renault for 2017 after debuting in F1 with the team at the start of the 2016 season, during which he scored his first World Championship points at the Malaysian Grand Prix.
Patrick Faure | Patrick Faure (] ) (born May 12, 1946) is a French businessman.  His best-known role was in motorsport, as President of Renault Sport and Renault F1 Team.
response.summary_2: The Force India VJM09 was driven by Le Mans winner Nico Hülkenberg, a German professional racing driver currently racing for the Renault Sport F1 Team.
